title: Model with composite functions
description: Given two basic functions that model a real-world situation, compose them in order to model a more complex situation.
topic_path: Get ready for contextual applications of differentiation

assessment_items.question_content:
Li Na studied her farm, where she modeled the following relationships.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$S$| Amount of rice noodles produced, $t$ | Consumption of soy sauce, $S(t)$
$R$ | Amount of rainfall, $x$ | Amount of rice grown, $R(x)$
$N$ | Amount of rice grown, $k$ | Amount of rice noodles produced, $N(k)$

**Which of the following expressions represents the consumption of soy sauce as a function of the amount of rice grown?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We are looking for a function that takes the **amount of rice grown** as its **input** and has **consumption of soy sauce** as its **output**.

$\text{rice}\rightarrow \boxed{\text{?}}\rightarrow\text{soy sauce}$

None of the functions relate those variables directly, but we can compose functions to relate them.

We need the function that takes the amount of rice grown as its input, which is $N$. We also need one that has the consumption of soy sauce as its output, which is $S$.

Does it make sense to compose the functions? Note that $N$ takes an amount of rice grown $k$ as input and then outputs the **amount of rice noodles produced**. Next, $S$ takes an **amount of rice noodles produced** as an input and then outputs the consumption of soy sauce.  Yes, the output from $N$ matches the input for $S$, so composing makes sense.

$\text{rice}\rightarrow \boxed{N}\rightarrow\text{rice noodles}\rightarrow \boxed{S}
\rightarrow\text{soy sauce}$

Therefore, if we take an amount of rice grown $k$ and input it into $N$, and then take the amount of rice noodles produced $N(k)$ and input it into $S$, we obtain the consumption of soy sauce as a function of the amount of rice grown, $S(N(k))$.

$k \rightarrow \boxed{N}\rightarrow N(k) \rightarrow \boxed{S}
\rightarrow S(N(k))$
In conclusion, the expression that represents the consumption of soy sauce as a function of the amount of rice grown is $S(N(k))$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Burian is a very prolific writer, able to write thousands of pages per day! His assistant modeled a few functional relationships below.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$P$ | Minutes of morning yoga, $x$ | Number of pages Burian writes, $P(x)$
$I$ | Number of pages Burian writes, $k$ | Number of ink cartridges used, $I(k)$
$S$| Number of ink cartridges used, $r$ | Number of times the assistant has to walk to the office supply shop, $S(r)$

**Which of the following expressions represents the number of ink cartridges used as a function of the minutes of morning yoga?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We are looking for a function that takes **the minutes of morning yoga** as its **input** and has **the number of ink cartridges used** as its **output**. 

$\text{yoga minutes}\rightarrow \boxed{\text{?}}\rightarrow\text{cartridges}$

None of the functions relate those variables directly, but we can compose functions to relate them.

We need the function that takes the minutes of morning yoga as its input, which is $P$. We also need one that has the number of ink cartridges used as its output, which is $I$.

Does it make sense to compose the functions? Note that $P$ takes the minutes of morning yoga $x$ as input and then outputs the **number of pages Burian writes**. Next, $I$ takes the **number of pages Burian writes as an input** and then outputs the number of ink cartridges used.  Yes, the output from $P$ matches the input for $I$, so composing makes sense.

$\text{yoga minutes}\rightarrow \boxed{P}\rightarrow\text{pages}\rightarrow \boxed{I}
\rightarrow\text{cartridges}$

Therefore, if we take the minutes of morning yoga $x$ and input it into $P$, and then take the number of pages Burian writes $P(x)$ and input it into $I$, we obtain the number of ink cartridges used as a function of the minutes of morning yoga, $I(P(x))$.

$x\rightarrow \boxed{P}\rightarrow P(x) \rightarrow \boxed{I}
\rightarrow I(P(x))$
In conclusion, the expression that represents the number of ink cartridges used as a function of the minutes of morning yoga is $I(P(x))$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mason is a captain of a tourist cruise to the Statue of Liberty. He modeled a few of the relationships.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$P$| Duration of the cruise, $r$ | Average number of photos taken by each passenger, $P(r)$
$S$ | Current of the river, $x$ | Speed of the ship, $S(x)$
$D$ | Speed of the ship, $k$ | Duration of the cruise, $D(k)$


**According to Mason's findings, which of the following represents the average number of photos taken per passenger as a function of the speed of the ship?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We are looking for a function that takes **the speed of the ship** as its **input** and has **the average number of photos taken per passenger** as its **output**.

$\text{speed}\rightarrow \boxed{\text{?}}\rightarrow\text{photos}$

None of the functions relate those variables directly, but we can compose functions to relate them.

We need the function that takes the speed of the ship as its input, which is $D$. We also need one that outputs the average number of photos taken per passenger, which is $P$.

Does it make sense to compose the functions? Note that $D$ takes the speed of the ship $k$ as input and then outputs the **duration of the cruise**. Next, $P$ takes the **duration of the cruise** as an input and then outputs the average number of photos taken per passenger. Yes, the output from $D$ matches the input for $P$, so composing makes sense.

$\text{speed}\rightarrow \boxed{D}\rightarrow\text{duration}\rightarrow \boxed{P}
\rightarrow\text{photos}$

Therefore, if we take the speed of the ship $k$ and input it into $D$, and then take the duration of the cruise $D(k)$ and input it into $P$, we obtain the average number of photos taken per passenger as a function of the speed of the ship, $P(D(k))$.

$k\rightarrow \boxed{D}\rightarrow D(k)\rightarrow \boxed{P}
\rightarrow P(D(k))$
In conclusion, the expression that represents the average number of photos taken per passenger as a function of the speed of the ship is $P(D(k))$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A statistician observed the health systems of several countries. She modeled a few relationships.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$C$ | Average daily consumption of cigarettes per person, $x$ | Probability that a person is sick, $C(x)$
$S$ | Probability that a person is sick, $k$ | The country's health expenses per person, $S(k)$
$P$| The country's health expenses per person, $r$ | The price of basic health insurance, $P(r)$

**According to the statistician's findings, which of the following represents the price of basic health insurance as a function of the probability that a person is sick?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We are looking for a function that takes the **probability that a person is sick** as its **input** and has the **price of basic health insurance** as its **output**.

$\text{sickness probability}\rightarrow \boxed{\text{?}}\rightarrow\text{insurance price}$

None of the functions relate those variables directly, but we can compose functions to relate them.

We need the function that takes the probability that a person is sick as its input, which is $S$. We also need one that has the price of basic health insurance as its output, which is $P$.

Does it make sense to compose the functions? Note that $S$ takes the probability that a person is sick $k$ as input and then outputs the country's **health expenses per person**. Next, $P$ takes the country's **health expenses per person** as an input and then outputs the price of basic health insurance. Yes, the output from $S$ matches the input for $P$, so composing makes sense.

$\text{sickness probability}\rightarrow \boxed{S}\rightarrow\text{health expenses}\rightarrow \boxed{P}
\rightarrow\text{insurance price}$

Therefore, if we take the probability that a person is sick $k$ and input it into $S$, and then take the country's health expenses per person $S(k)$ and input it into $P$, we obtain the price of basic health insurance as a function of the probability that a person is sick, $P(S(k))$.

$k\rightarrow \boxed{S}\rightarrow S(k) \rightarrow \boxed{P}
\rightarrow P(S(k))$
In conclusion, the expression that represents the price of basic health insurance as a function of the probability that a person is sick is $P(S(k))$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Andrea doubles as a professional pianist and a dedicated mother. She modeled a few relationships.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$A$| Hours Andrea practices before a concert, $x$ | Minutes of applause Andrea receives after the concert, $A(x)$
$S$ | Hours Andrea sleeps, $k$ | Hours Andrea practices before a concert, $S(k)$
$B$ | Number of times Andrea's baby wakes up at night, $r$ | Hours Andrea sleeps, $B(r)$


**Which of the following represents the hours Andrea practices before a concert as a function of the number of times her baby wakes up at night?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We are looking for a function that takes the number of **times Andrea's baby wakes up** at night as its **input** and has the **hours Andrea practices** as its **output**.

$\text{awakenings}\rightarrow \boxed{\text{?}}\rightarrow\text{practice hours}$

None of the functions relate those variables directly, but we can compose functions to relate them.

We need the function that takes the number of times Andrea's baby wakes up at night as its input, which is $B$. We also need one that has the hours Andrea practices before a concert as its output, which is $S$.

Does it make sense to compose the functions? Note that $B$ takes the number of times Andrea's baby wakes up at night $r$ as input and then outputs the **hours Andrea sleeps**. Next, $S$ takes the **hours Andrea sleeps** as an input and then outputs the hours she practices before a concert. Yes, the output from $B$ matches the input for $S$, so composing makes sense.

$\text{awakenings}\rightarrow \boxed{B}\rightarrow\text{sleep hours}\rightarrow \boxed{S}
\rightarrow\text{practice hours}$

Therefore, if we take the number of times Andrea's baby wakes up at night $r$ and input it into $B$, and then take the hours Andrea sleeps $B(r)$ and input it into $S$, we obtain the hours Andrea practices before a concert as a function of the number of times her baby wakes up at night, $S(B(r))$.

$r \rightarrow \boxed{B}\rightarrow B(r) \rightarrow \boxed{S}
\rightarrow S(B(r))$
In conclusion, the expression that represents the hours Andrea practices before a concert as a function of the number of times her baby wakes up at night is $S(B(r))$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Robert is a stuntman planning to jump off a bicycle ramp and over an array of trucks. He modeled a few relationships.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$S$| Height of the ramp, $r$ | Speed at the edge of the ramp, $S(r)$
$D$ | Speed at the edge of the ramp, $k$ | Distance of the jump, $D(k)$
$T$ | Distance of the jump, $x$ | Number of trucks Robert can jump over, $T(x)$

**Which of the following represents the distance of Robert's jump as a function of the height of the ramp?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We are looking for a function that takes the **height of the ramp** as its **input** and has the **distance of Robert's jump** as its **output**.

$\text{ramp height}\rightarrow \boxed{\text{?}}\rightarrow\text{jump distance}$

None of the functions relate those variables directly, but we can compose functions to relate them.

We need the function that takes the height of the ramp as its input, which is $S$. We also need one that has the distance of Robert's jump as its output, which is $D$.

Does it make sense to compose the functions? Note that $S$ takes the height of the ramp $r$ as input and then outputs **speed at edge of ramp**. Next, $D$ takes the **speed at the edge of the ramp** as an input and then outputs the distance of the jump. Yes, the output from $S$ matches the input for $D$, so composing makes sense.

$\text{ramp height}\rightarrow \boxed{S}\rightarrow\text{speed}\rightarrow \boxed{D}
\rightarrow\text{jump distance}$

Therefore, if we take the height of the ramp $r$ and input it into $S$, and then take speed at the edge of the ramp $S(r)$ and put it into $D$, we obtain the distance of Robert's jump as a function of the height of the ramp, $D(S(r))$.

$r \rightarrow \boxed{S}\rightarrow S(r) \rightarrow \boxed{D}
\rightarrow D(S(r))$
In conclusion, the expression that represents the distance of Robert's jump as a function of the height of the ramp is $D(S(r))$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Prachi, the office manager at a private practice, modeled a few relationships.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$P$| Time of day, $t$ | People in waiting room, $P(t)$
$L$ | People in waiting room, $x$ | Average length of visit, $L(x)$
$W$ | People in waiting room, $n$ | Patient's waiting time, $W(n)$

**Which of the following represents a patient's waiting time as a function of the time of day?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We are looking for a function that takes the **time of day** as its **input** and has **patient's waiting time** as its **output**.

$\text{time of day}\rightarrow \boxed{\text{?}}\rightarrow\text{wait time}$

None of the functions relate those variables directly, but we can compose functions to relate them.

We need the function that takes the time of day as its input, which is $P$. We also need one that has the waiting time as its output, which is $W$.

Does it make sense to compose the functions? Note that $P$ takes the time of day $t$ as input and then outputs the number of **people in the waiting room**. Next, $W$ takes the number of **people in the waiting room** as an input and then outputs the patient's waiting time. Yes, the output from $P$ matches the input for $W$, so composing makes sense.

$\text{time of day}\rightarrow \boxed{P}\rightarrow\text{people waiting}\rightarrow \boxed{W}
\rightarrow\text{wait time}$

Therefore, if we take the time of day $t$ and input it into $P$, and then take the number of people in the waiting room $P(t)$ and put it into $W$, we obtain the patient's waiting time as a function of the time of day, $W(P(t))$.

$t \rightarrow \boxed{P}\rightarrow P(t)\rightarrow \boxed{W}
\rightarrow W(P(t))$
In conclusion, the expression that represents a patient's waiting time as a function of the time of day is  $W(P(t))$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Carter plays on a football team. He modeled a few relationships.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$N$| Winning percentage, $w$ | Average number of fans per  game, $N(w)$
$W$ | Average daily practice time, $x$ | Winning percentage, $W(x)$
$P$ | Number of rainy days during the season, $r$ | Average daily practice time, $P(r)$

**Which of the following represents the winning percentage of Carter's team as a function of the number of rainy days during the season?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We are looking for a function that takes the **number of rainy days** as its **input** and has **winning percentage** as its **output**.

$\text{rainy days}\rightarrow \boxed{\text{?}}\rightarrow\text{winning percentage}$

None of the functions relate those variables directly, but we can compose functions to relate them.

We need the function that takes the number of rainy days during the season as its input, which is $P$. We also need one that has the winning percentage as its output, which is $W$.

Does it make sense to compose the functions? Note that $P$ takes the number of rainy days $r$ as input and then outputs the **average daily practice time**. Next, $W$ takes the **average daily practice time** as an input and then outputs the winning percentage. Yes, the output from $P$ matches the input for $W$, so composing makes sense.

$\text{rainy days}\rightarrow \boxed{P}\rightarrow\text{practice time}\rightarrow \boxed{W}
\rightarrow\text{winning percentage}$

Therefore, if we take the number of rainy days during the season $r$ and input it into $P$, and then take the average daily practice time $P(r)$ and put it into $W$, we obtain the winning percentage of Carter's team as a function of the number of rainy days during the season, $W(P(r))$.

$r \rightarrow \boxed{P}\rightarrow P(r) \rightarrow \boxed{W}
\rightarrow W(P(r))$
In conclusion, the expression that represents the winning percentage of Carter's team as a function of the number of rainy days during the season is $W(P(r))$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Deniz studied the park near her home, where she saw and modeled the following relationships.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$B$ | Height of a tree, $x$ | Number of birds nesting in that tree, $B(x)$
$H$ | Average temperature at a specific location, $k$ | Height of a tree at that location, $H(k)$
$T$| Altitude of a specific location, $r$ | Average temperature at that location, $T(r)$

**The expression $B(H(k))$ represents which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When considering the meaning of the expression $B(H(k))$, we can work our way from the inside out.

$k \rightarrow \boxed{H}\rightarrow H(k) \rightarrow \boxed{B}
\rightarrow B(H(k))$

*Note: the composition will only make sense if the output of $H$ matches the input of $B$.*

What is the input of the composed function? What is the output?

First, let's consider the expression $H(k)$ which serves as the input to function $B$. 

Function $H$ inputs  the *average temperature* at a specific location, $k$,  and outputs $H(k)$, which represents the *height of the tree* at that location.

$\text{average temperature} \rightarrow \boxed{H}\rightarrow \text{tree height} \rightarrow \boxed{B}
\rightarrow B(H(k))$

Function $B$ inputs a *tree height*, and so $H(k)$ is an acceptable input.  

The final output, $B(H(k))$, represents the *number of birds nesting in that tree*.

$\text{average temperature} \rightarrow \boxed{H}\rightarrow \text{tree height} \rightarrow \boxed{B}
\rightarrow \text{number of birds}$
The expression $B(H(k))$ represents the number of birds nesting in a tree as a function of the average temperature at that location.

choices.content: The height of a tree as a function of its altitude
The average temperature as a function of the altitude at that location
The number of birds nesting in a tree as a function of the average temperature at that location

assessment_items.question_content:
Rafael has a solar-powered video game console. He saw and modeled the following relationships.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$D$ | The time of year, $x$ | Hours of daylight, $D(x)$
$E$ | Hours of daylight, $k$ | Amount of solar energy gained over the day, $E(k)$
$G$| Amount of solar energy, $r$ | Hours of video games Rafael can play, $G(r)$

**The expression  $G(E(k))$ represents which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When considering the meaning of the expression $G(E(k))$, we can work our way from the inside out.

$k \rightarrow \boxed{E}\rightarrow E(k) \rightarrow \boxed{G}
\rightarrow G(E(k))$

*Note: the composition will only make sense if the output of $E$ matches the input of $G$.*

What is the input of the composed function? What is the output?

First, let's consider the expression $E(k)$ which serves as the input to function $G$. 

Function $E$ inputs $k$ *hours of daylight* and outputs $E(k)$, which represents *the amount of solar energy gained over the day*.

$\text{daylight hours} \rightarrow \boxed{E}\rightarrow \text{solar energy} \rightarrow \boxed{G}
\rightarrow G(E(k))$

Function $G$ inputs an *amount of solar energy*, and so $E(k)$ is an acceptable input.  

The final output, $G(E(k))$, represents *the hours of video games Rafael can play*.

$\text{daylight hours} \rightarrow \boxed{E}\rightarrow \text{solar energy} \rightarrow \boxed{G}
\rightarrow \text{game hours}$
The expression $G(E(k))$ represents the hours of video games Rafael can play as a function of the hours of daylight.

choices.content: The solar energy gained as a function of the time of year
The hours of video games Rafael can play as a function of the time of year
The hours of video games Rafael can play as a function of the hours of daylight

assessment_items.question_content:
Mason is a captain of a tourist cruise to the Statue of Liberty. He saw and modeled the following relationships.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$P$| Duration of the cruise, $r$ | Average number of photos taken by each passenger, $P(r)$
$D$ | Speed of the ship, $k$ | Duration of the cruise, $D(k)$
$S$ | Rate of the river current, $x$ | Speed of the ship, $S(x)$

**The expression $D(S(x))$ represents which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When considering the meaning of the expression $D(S(x))$, we can work our way from the inside out.

$x \rightarrow \boxed{S}\rightarrow S(x) \rightarrow \boxed{D}
\rightarrow D(S(x))$

*Note: the composition will only make sense if the output of $S$ matches the input of $D$.*

What is the input of the composed function? What is the output?

First, let's consider the expression $S(x)$ which serves as the input to function $D$. 

Function $S$ inputs *rate of the river current*, $x$, and outputs $S(x)$, which represents *the speed of the ship*.

$\text{current rate} \rightarrow \boxed{S}\rightarrow \text{ship speed} \rightarrow \boxed{D}
\rightarrow D(S(x))$

Function $D$ inputs *the speed of the ship*, and so $S(x)$ is an acceptable input.  

The final output, $D(S(x))$, represents *the duration of the cruise*.

$\text{current rate} \rightarrow \boxed{S}\rightarrow \text{ship speed} \rightarrow \boxed{D}
\rightarrow \text{cruise duration}$
The expression $D(S(x))$ represents the duration of the cruise as a function of the rate of the river current.

choices.content: The duration of the cruise as a function of the rate of the river current
The speed of the cruise ship as a function of the rate of the river current
The average number of photos taken per passenger as a function of the speed of the ship

assessment_items.question_content:
A statistician observed the health systems of several countries. She modeled a few relationships.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$C$ | Average daily consumption of cigarettes per person, $x$ | Probability that a person is sick, $C(x)$
$S$ | Probability that a person is sick, $k$ | The country's health expenses per person, $S(k)$
$P$| The country's health expenses per person, $r$ | The price of basic health insurance, $P(r)$

**The expression $S(C(x))$ represents which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When considering the meaning of the expression $S(C(x))$, we can work our way from the inside out.

$x \rightarrow \boxed{C}\rightarrow C(x) \rightarrow \boxed{S}
\rightarrow S(C(x))$

*Note: the composition will only make sense if the output of $C$ matches the input of $S$.*

What is the input of the composed function? What is the output?

First, let's consider the expression $C(x)$ which serves as the input to function $S$. 

Function $C$ inputs an average daily consumption of cigarettes per person, $x$, and outputs $C(x)$, which represents the *probability that a person is sick*.

$\text{daily cigarettes} \rightarrow \boxed{C}\rightarrow \text{sickness probability} \rightarrow \boxed{S}
\rightarrow S(C(x))$

Function $S$ inputs the *probability that a person is sick*, and so $C(x)$ is an acceptable input.  

The final output, $S(C(x))$, represents *the country's health expenses per person*.

$\text{daily cigarettes} \rightarrow \boxed{C}\rightarrow \text{sickness probability} \rightarrow \boxed{S}
\rightarrow \text{health expenses}$
The expression $S(C(x))$ represents the country's health expenses as a function of the daily cigarette consumption.

choices.content: The probability of being sick as a function of the daily cigarette consumption
The country's health expenses as a function of the daily cigarette consumption
The price of basic health insurance as a function of the per person daily cigarette consumption

assessment_items.question_content:
Andrea doubles as a professional pianist and a dedicated mother. She modeled a few relationships.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$A$| Hours Andrea practices before a concert, $x$ | Minutes of applause Andrea receives after the concert, $A(x)$
$S$ | Hours Andrea sleeps, $k$ | Hours Andrea practices before a concert, $S(k)$
$B$ | Number of times Andrea's baby wakes up at night, $r$ | Hours Andrea sleeps, $B(r)$

**The expression $A(S(k))$ represents which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When considering the meaning of the expression $A(S(k))$, we can work our way from the inside out.

$k \rightarrow \boxed{S}\rightarrow S(k) \rightarrow \boxed{A}
\rightarrow A(S(k))$

*Note: the composition will only make sense if the output of $S$ matches the input of $A$.*

What is the input of the composed function? What is the output?

First, let's consider the expression $S(k)$ which serves as the input to function $A$. 

Function $S$ inputs the hours Andrea sleeps, $k$, and outputs $S(k)$, which represents the *hours Andrea practices* before the concert.

$\text{sleep hours} \rightarrow \boxed{S}\rightarrow \text{practice hours} \rightarrow \boxed{A}
\rightarrow A(S(k))$

Function $A$ inputs the *hours Andrea practices* before the concert, and so $S(k)$ is an acceptable input.  

The final output, $A(S(k))$, represents *the minutes of applause Andrea receives after the concert*.

$\text{sleep hours} \rightarrow \boxed{S}\rightarrow \text{practice hours} \rightarrow \boxed{A}
\rightarrow \text{applause minutes}$
The expression $A(S(k))$ represents the minutes of applause Andrea receives after the concert as a function of the hours she sleeps.

choices.content: The hours Andrea practices before a concert as a function of the hours she sleeps
The minutes of applause Andrea receives after a concert as a function of the hours she sleeps
The minutes of applause Andrea receives after a concert as a function of the number of times her baby wakes up at night

assessment_items.question_content:
Robert is a stuntman planning to jump off a bicycle ramp and over an array of trucks. He modeled a few relationships.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$S$| Height of the ramp, $r$ | Speed at the edge of the ramp, $S(r)$
$D$ | Speed at the edge of the ramp, $k$ | Distance of the jump, $D(k)$
$T$ | Distance of the jump, $x$ | Number of trucks Robert can jump over, $T(x)$

**The expression $T(D(k))$ represents which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When considering the meaning of the expression $T(D(k))$, we can work our way from the inside out.

$k \rightarrow \boxed{D}\rightarrow D(k) \rightarrow \boxed{T}
\rightarrow T(D(k))$

*Note: the composition will only make sense if the output of $D$ matches the input of $T$.*

What is the input of the composed function? What is the output?

First, let's consider the expression $D(k)$ which serves as the input to function $T$. 

Function $D$ inputs the speed $k$ at the edge of the ramp, and outputs $D(k)$, which represents the *distance of the jump*.

$\text{speed at ramp edge} \rightarrow \boxed{D}\rightarrow \text{jump distance} \rightarrow \boxed{T}
\rightarrow T(D(k))$

Function $T$ inputs the *distance of the jump*, so $D(k)$ is an acceptable input.  

The final output, $T(D(k))$, represents the *number of trucks* Robert can jump over.

$\text{speed at ramp edge} \rightarrow \boxed{D}\rightarrow \text{jump distance} \rightarrow \boxed{T}
\rightarrow \text{trucks}$
The expression $T(D(k))$ represents the number of trucks Robert can jump over as a function of the speed at the edge of the ramp.

choices.content: The distance of the jump as a function of the height of the ramp
The speed at the edge of the ramp as a function of the distance of the jump
The number of trucks Robert can jump over as a function of the speed at the edge of the ramp

assessment_items.question_content:
Prachi, the office manager at a private practice, modeled a few relationships.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$P$| Time of day, $t$ | People in waiting room, $P(t)$
$W$ | People in waiting room, $n$ | Patient's waiting time, $W(n)$
$L$ | People in waiting room, $x$ | Average length of visit, $L(x)$

**The expression $L(P(t))$ represents which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When considering the meaning of the expression $L(P(t))$, we can work our way from the inside out.

$t \rightarrow \boxed{P}\rightarrow P(t) \rightarrow \boxed{L}
\rightarrow L(P(t))$

*Note: the composition will only make sense if the output of $P$ matches the input of $L$.*

What is the input of the composed function? What is the output?

First, let's consider the expression $P(t)$ which serves as the input to function $L$. 

Function $P$ inputs the time of day, $t$, and outputs $P(t)$, which represents *the number of people in the waiting room*.

$\text{time of day} \rightarrow \boxed{P}\rightarrow \text{people waiting} \rightarrow \boxed{L}
\rightarrow L(P(t))$

Function $L$ inputs the *number of people in the waiting room*, and so $P(t)$ is an acceptable input.  

The final output, $L(P(t))$, represents *the average length of a patient visit*.

$\text{time of day} \rightarrow \boxed{P}\rightarrow \text{people waiting} \rightarrow \boxed{L}
\rightarrow \text{length of visit}$
The expression $L(P(t))$ represents the average length of a patient visit as a function of the time of day.

choices.content: A patient's waiting time as a function of the time of day
The average length of a patient visit as a function of the time of day
The number of people in the waiting room as a function of the time of day

assessment_items.question_content:
Carter plays on a football team. He modeled a few relationships.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$N$| Winning percentage, $w$ | Average number of fans per game, $N(w)$
$W$ | Average daily practice time, $x$ | Winning percentage, $W(x)$
$P$ | Number of rainy days, $r$ | Average daily practice time, $P(r)$

**The expression $N(W(x))$ represents which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When considering the meaning of the expression $N(W(x))$, we can work our way from the inside out.

$x \rightarrow \boxed{W}\rightarrow W(x) \rightarrow \boxed{N} \rightarrow N(W(x))$

*Note: the composition will only make sense if the output of $W$ matches the input of $N$.*

What is the input of the composed function? What is the output?

First, let's consider the expression $W(x)$ which serves as the input to function $N$. 

Function $W$ inputs the average daily practice time, $x$, and outputs $W(x)$, which represents *the winning percentage of Carter's team*.

$\text{practice time} \rightarrow \boxed{W}\rightarrow \text{winning percentage} \rightarrow \boxed{N} \rightarrow N(W(x))$

Function $N$ inputs the *winning percentage of Carter's team*, and so $W(x)$ is an acceptable input.  

The final output, $N(W(x))$, represents *the average number of fans per game*.

$\text{practice time} \rightarrow \boxed{W}\rightarrow \text{winning percentage} \rightarrow \boxed{N} \rightarrow \text{fans per game}$
The expression $N(W(x))$ represents the average number of fans at games as a function of the average daily practice time.

choices.content: The team's winning percentage as a function of the average daily practice time
The average number of fans per game as a function of the number of rainy days in a season
The average number of fans per game as a function of the team's average daily practice time

assessment_items.question_content:
Oil is leaking from a damaged shipping vessel stranded at sea. 

The captain estimates that the costs, $C$, in millions of dollars, to clean a $t$ ton oil spill can be modeled by the  function $C(t)=1.5t+2$.  

The amount, $S$, of oil, in tons, that has been spilled in $d$ days is given by the function $S(d)=355d$.  

**Which expression models the costs of cleaning up a $d$ day oil spill?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We are looking for a function that takes the **duration**, in days of the oil spill, as its **input** and has the **costs** to clean as its **output**. 

$\text{duration}\rightarrow \boxed{\text{?}}\rightarrow\text{cost}$

Neither of the functions relate those variables directly, but we can compose functions to relate them.

Function $C$ gives the cleaning costs, $C(t)$, as a function of the amount, $t$, of oil spilled.  

Function $S$ gives the amount, $S(d)$, of oil spilled as a function of the duration, $d$, of the spill in days.

$\text{duration}\rightarrow \boxed{S}\rightarrow\text{oil amount}\rightarrow \boxed{C}
\rightarrow\text{cost}$

Therefore, $C(S(d))$ will give the cleaning costs of cleaning a $d$ day oil spill.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

$d\rightarrow \boxed{S}\rightarrow S(d)\rightarrow \boxed{C}
\rightarrow C(S(d))$

Let's find $C(S(d))$ by substituting the formula for $S(d)$ into function $C$ as shown below.

$\begin{align}{C(}\purpleD{S(d)}{)}&= 1.5(\purpleD{S(d)})+2\\
\\
&=1.5(\purpleD{355d})+2
\end{align}$

We can simplify this as follows:

$\begin{align}\phantom{C(S(d)}&=1.5(355d)+2\\
\\
&=532.5d+2
\end{align}$
An expression that models the costs of cleaning up a $d$ day oil spill is $532.5d+2$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Moses has a bakery, and he is expecting a new shipment of 
flour today. 

The number of loaves, $N$, that Moses will bake today given that he receives $k$ kilograms of flour is given by the function $N(k)=10+3k$.  

The income, $I$, in dollars, that Moses makes from baking $x$ loaves of bread is given by $I(x)=6x$.  

**Which expression models Moses's income for the day if he receives $k$ kilograms of flour?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We are looking for a function that takes the amount of **flour**, in kilograms, as its **input** and has Moses's **income** as its **output**. 

$\text{flour}\rightarrow \boxed{\text{?}}\rightarrow\text{income}$

Neither of the functions relate those variables directly, but we can compose functions to relate them.

Function $I$ gives Moses's income, $I(x)$, as a function of the number, $x$, of loaves baked.  

Function $N$ gives the number, $N(k)$, of loaves baked as a function of the amount, $k$, of flour, in kilograms, received in the shipment.

$\text{flour}\rightarrow \boxed{N}\rightarrow\text{loaves baked}\rightarrow \boxed{I}
\rightarrow\text{income}$

Therefore, $I(N(k))$ will give Moses's income for the day if he receives $k$ kilograms of flour.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

$k \rightarrow \boxed{N}\rightarrow N(k)\rightarrow \boxed{I}
\rightarrow I(N(k))$

Let's find $I(N(k))$ by substituting the formula for $N(k)$ into function $I$ as shown below.

$\begin{align}{I(}\purpleD{N(k)}{)}&= 6(\purpleD{N(k)})\\
\\
&=6(\purpleD{10+3k})
\end{align}$

We can simplify this as follows:

$\begin{align}\phantom{C(S(d)}&=6(10+3k)\\
\\
&=60+18k
\end{align}$
An expression that models Moses's income for the day if he receives $k$ kilograms of flour is $60+18k$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Skyler climbs a watchtower to guard against forest fires. 

The function $R(h)=\sqrt{13h}$ gives the distance, in kilometers, that Skyler can see when their eyes are $h$ meters above the ground.

The function $A(d)=\pi d^2$ gives the area, in square kilometers, that Skyler can guard when they can see a distance of $d$ kilometers.

**Which expression models the area Skyler can guard when their eyes are $h$ meters above the ground?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We are looking for a function that takes the **height** of Skyler's eyes, in meters, as its **input** and has the **guarded area** as its **output**. 

$\text{height}\rightarrow \boxed{\text{?}}\rightarrow\text{guarded area}$

Neither of the functions relate those variables directly, but we can compose functions to relate them.

Function $R$ gives the distance, $R(h)$, Skyler can see as a function of the height, $h$, of their eyes.  

Function $A$ gives the area Skyler can guard, $A(d)$, as a function of the distance, $d$, in kilometers, Skyler can see.

$\text{height}\rightarrow \boxed{R}\rightarrow\text{distance seen}\rightarrow \boxed{A}
\rightarrow\text{guarded area}$

Therefore, $A(R(h))$ will give the area Skyler can guard if their eyes are $h$ meters from the ground.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

$h \rightarrow \boxed{R}\rightarrow R(h) \rightarrow \boxed{A}
\rightarrow A(R(h))$

Let's find $A(R(h))$ by substituting the formula for $R(h)$ into function $A$ as shown below.

$\begin{align}A(\purpleD{R(h)})&= \pi(\purpleD{R(h)})^2\\\\
&=\pi(\purpleD{\sqrt{13h}})^2
\end{align}$

We can simplify this as follows:

$\begin{align}\phantom{C(S(d)}&=\pi(\purpleD{\sqrt{13h}})^2\\\\
&=\pi (13h)\\\\
&=13\pi h
\end{align}$
An expression that models the area Skyler can guard when their eyes are $h$ meters above the ground is $13\pi h$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A submarine is diving toward the bottom of the ocean. 

The depth $D$ of the submarine, in feet below sea level, after $t$ minutes have elapsed is given by the function $D(t)=20+16t$.    

The pressure, $P$, in pounds per square inch, exerted on the submarine at a depth of $x$ feet is given by the function $P(x)=14.7+0.445x$.  

**Which expression models the pressure exerted on the submarine as a function of the elapsed time, $t$, in minutes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We are looking for a function that takes the **elapsed time** as its **input** and has the **pressure** as its **output**. 

$\text{elapsed time}\rightarrow \boxed{\text{?}}\rightarrow\text{pressure}$

Neither of the functions relate those variables directly, but we can compose functions to relate them.

Function $P$ gives the pressure, $P(x)$, on the submarine as a function of the depth of the submarine, $x$.  

Function $D$ gives the depth, $D(t)$, of the submarine as a function of elapsed time, $t$, in minutes.

$\text{elapsed time}\rightarrow \boxed{D}\rightarrow\text{depth}\rightarrow \boxed{P}
\rightarrow\text{pressure}$

Therefore, $P(D(t))$ will give the pressure exerted on the submarine as a function of the elapsed time $t$, in minutes.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

$t \rightarrow \boxed{D}\rightarrow D(t) \rightarrow \boxed{P}
\rightarrow P(D(t))$

Let's find $P(D(t))$ by substituting the formula for $D(t)$ into function $P$ as shown below.

$\begin{align}{P(}\purpleD{D(t)}{)}&= 14.7+0.445(\purpleD{D(t)})\\
\\
&=14.7+0.445(\purpleD{20+16t})
\end{align}$

We can simplify this as follows:

$\begin{align}\phantom{P(D(t)}&=14.7+0.445(20+16t)\\
\\
&=14.7+8.9+7.12t\\
\\
&=23.6+7.12t
\end{align}$
An expression that models the pressure exerted on the submarine as a function of the elapsed time, $t$, in minutes is $23.6+7.12t$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Duru has an ice-cream stand, which she opens on warm days.

When open for business, her daily profit, $P$, is a function of the number, $n$, of cones she sells and can be modeled by the function $P(n)=2n-20$.   Additionally, she finds that the number, $C$, of cones she sells is a function of the average temperature, $t$, in degrees Celsius $(^\circ\text{C})$, and can be modeled by the function $C(t)=50+10(t-10)$.  

**Which expression gives Duru's profit on a day with an average temperature of $t^\circ\text{C}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We are looking for a function that takes the **average temperature** as its **input** and has the **profit** as its **output**. 

$\text{average temperature}\rightarrow \boxed{\text{?}}\rightarrow\text{profit}$

Neither of the functions relate those variables directly, but we can compose functions to relate them.

Function $P$ gives Duru's daily profits, $P(n)$, as a function of the number of cones, $n$, she sells that day.  

Function $C$ gives the number of cones sold, $C(t)$, on a day with an average temperature of $t^\circ\text{C}$.

$\text{average temperature}\rightarrow \boxed{C}\rightarrow\text{cones}\rightarrow \boxed{P}
\rightarrow\text{profit}$

Therefore, $P(C(t))$ will give the Duru's daily profits on a day with an average temperature of $t^\circ\text{C}$.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

$t \rightarrow \boxed{C}\rightarrow C(t) \rightarrow \boxed{P}
\rightarrow P(C(t))$

Let's find $P(C(t))$ by substituting the formula for $C(t)$ into function $P$ as shown below.

$\begin{align}{P(}\purpleD{C(t)}{)}&= 2\left(\purpleD{C(t)}\right)-20\\\\
&=2\left(\purpleD{50+10(t-10)}\right)-20
\end{align}$

We can simplify this as follows:

$\begin{align}\phantom{C(S(d)}&=2\left(50+10(t-10)\right)-20\\
\\
&=2(50+10t-100)-20\\
\\
&=2(10t-50)-20\\
\\
&=20t-100-20\\
\\
&=20t-120
\end{align}$
An expression that gives Duru's profit on a day with an average temperature of $t^\circ\text{C}$ is $20t-120$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ayumi plans to go blueberry picking before school.

The amount, $B$, of blueberries, in cups, that Ayumi picks given that she starts picking $t$ hours after midnight, is given by the function $B(t)=2(8-t)$.  

After school, she plans to bake blueberry muffins with her pickings.  The number, $M$, of muffins that she can bake with $n$ cups of blueberries is given by the function $M(n)=12n$.  

**Which expression gives the number of muffins Ayumi can bake given that she starts picking $t$ hours after midnight?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We are looking for a function that takes the **hours** after midnight as its **input** and has the number of **muffins** as its **output**. 

$\text{hours}\rightarrow \boxed{\text{?}}\rightarrow\text{muffins}$

Neither of the functions relate those variables directly, but we can compose functions to relate them.

Function $M$ gives the number, $M(n)$, of muffins Ayumi can bake given that she picks $n$ cups of blueberries.  

Function $B$ gives the amount, $B(t)$, blueberries that Ayumi picks  given that she starts picking $t$ hours after midnight.

$\text{hours}\rightarrow \boxed{B}\rightarrow\text{blueberries}\rightarrow \boxed{M}
\rightarrow\text{muffins}$

Therefore, $M(B(t))$ will give the number of muffins Ayumi can bake given that she starts picking $t$ hours after midnight.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

$t \rightarrow \boxed{B}\rightarrow B(t) \rightarrow \boxed{M}
\rightarrow M(B(t))$

Let's find $M(B(t))$ by substituting the formula for $B(t)$ into function $M$ as shown below.

$\begin{align}{M(}\purpleD{B(t)}{)}&= 12(\purpleD{B(t)})\\\\
&=12(\purpleD{2(8-t)})
\end{align}$

We can simplify this as follows:

$\begin{align}\phantom{C(S(d)}&=12(2(8-t))\\\\
&=24(8-t)\\\\
&=192-24t
\end{align}$
An expression that gives the number of muffins Ayumi can bake given that she starts picking $t$ hours after midnight  is $192-24t$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Anais, a math teacher, noticed that the average grade, $G$, on an exam given that $n$ students watch the related video can be given by the function $G(n)=50+1.5n$. She also noticed that the number, $S$, of students who watch an $m$ minute video can be modeled by the function $S(m)=30-m$.

**Which expression gives the average grade on an exam if the related video is $m$ minutes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We are looking for a function that takes the **video length** as its **input** and has the **average grade** on the exam as its **output**. 

$\text{video length}\rightarrow \boxed{\text{?}}\rightarrow\text{average grade}$

Neither of the functions relate those variables directly, but we can compose functions to relate them.

Function $G$ gives the average exam grade, $G(n)$, as a function of number, $n$, of students who watch the related video. 

Function $S$ gives the number, $S(m)$, of students who watch a video given that it is $m$ minutes long.

$\text{video length}\rightarrow \boxed{S}\rightarrow\text{students}\rightarrow \boxed{G}
\rightarrow\text{average grade}$

Therefore, $G(S(m))$ will give the average exam grade given that the related video is $m$ minutes long.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

$m \rightarrow \boxed{S}\rightarrow S(m) \rightarrow \boxed{G}
\rightarrow G(S(m))$

Let's find $G(S(m))$ by substituting the formula for $S(m)$ into function $G$ as shown below.

$\begin{align}{G(}\purpleD{S(m)}{)}&= 50+1.5(\purpleD{S(m)})\\\\
&=50+1.5(\purpleD{30-m})
\end{align}$

We can simplify this as follows:

$\begin{align}\phantom{C(S(d)}&=50+1.5({30-m})\\
\\
&=50+45-1.5m\\
\\
&=95-1.5m
\end{align}$
An expression that gives the average grade on an exam if the related video is $m$ minutes is $95-1.5m$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Laura runs during her lunch break.  

The average speed $S$, in kilometers per hour, at which Laura runs depends on the temperature $t$,  in degrees Celsius $(^{\circ}\text{C})$, at the start of her run and can be modeled by the function $S(t)=6+0.2(25-t)$.  

The distance, $D$, in kilometers, that she can run in $30$ minutes given that her average speed is $x$ kilometers per hour can be modeled by the function $D(x)=0.5x$.  

** Which expression models the distance that Laura runs in $30$ minutes given that it is $t^\circ{\text{C}}$  outside at the start of her run?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We are looking for a function that takes the **temperature** as its **input** and has the **distance** Laura runs as its **output**. 

$\text{temperature}\rightarrow \boxed{\text{?}}\rightarrow\text{distance}$

Neither of the functions relate those variables directly, but we can compose functions to relate them.

Function $D$ gives the distance, $D(x)$, that Laura can run in $30$ minutes as a function of her average speed, $x$, in kilometers per hour.  

Function $S$ gives Laura's average speed, $S(t)$, as a function of the temperature, $t$, at which she starts the run.

$\text{temperature}\rightarrow \boxed{S}\rightarrow\text{speed}\rightarrow \boxed{D}
\rightarrow\text{distance}$

Therefore, $D(S(t))$ will give the distance that Laura runs in $30$ minutes given that it is $t$ degrees outside at the start of her run.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

$t \rightarrow \boxed{S}\rightarrow S(t) \rightarrow \boxed{D}
\rightarrow D(S(t))$

Let's find $D(S(t))$ by substituting the formula for $S(t)$ into function $D$ as shown below.

$\begin{align}{D(}\purpleD{S(t)}{)}&= 0.5(\purpleD{S(t)})\\
\\
&=0.5(\purpleD{6+0.2(25-t)})
\end{align}$

We can simplify this as follows:

$\begin{align}\phantom{D(S(t)}&=0.5(6+0.2(25-t))\\
\\
&=0.5(6+5-0.2t)\\
\\
&=0.5(11-0.2t)\\
\\
&=5.5-0.1t
\end{align}$
An expression that models the distance that Laura runs in $30$ minutes given that it is $t^\circ{\text{C}}$  outside at the start of her run is $5.5-0.1t$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Deniz studied the park near her home, where she saw and modeled the following relationships.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$B$ | Height of a tree, $x$ | Number of birds nesting in that tree, $B(x)$
$H$ | Average temperature at a specific location, $k$ | Height of a tree at that location, $H(k)$
$T$| Altitude of a specific location, $r$ | Average temperature at that location, $T(r)$

**Which $2$ of the following composed functions make sense in this context?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The inner function's output becomes the input to the outer function. So the composition will only make sense if the output of the inner function matches the input of outer function.

Let's look at whether the outer function gets the correct input.

Composed function | Inner function | Output of inner function | Outer function | Input of outer function | Outer function has the right input?
- | - | - | - | - | -
$B(H(k))$ | $H$ | Height of a tree at that location | $B$ | Height of a tree | Yes
$H(T(r))$ | $T$ | Average temperature at that location | $H$ | Average temperature at a specific location | Yes
$T(H(k))$ | $H$ | Height of a tree at that location | $T$ | Altitude of a specific location | No
$H(B(r))$ | $B$ | Number of birds nesting in that tree | $H$ | Average temperature at a specific location | No

Neither $T(H(k))$ nor $H(B(r))$ get inputs that are within their domain, so neither makes sense in this context.

In conclusion, the following two composed functions make sense in this context:

- $B(H(k))$
- $H(T(r))$

assessment_items.question_content:
Rafael has a solar-powered video game console. He saw and modeled the following relationships.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$D$ | The time of year, $x$ | Hours of daylight, $D(x)$
$E$ | Hours of daylight, $k$ | Amount of solar energy gained over the day, $E(k)$
$G$| Amount of solar energy, $r$ | Hours of video games Rafael can play, $G(r)$

**Which $2$ of the following composed functions make sense in this context?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The inner function's output becomes the input to the outer function. So the composition will only make sense if the output of the inner function matches the input of outer function.

Let's look at whether the outer function gets the correct input.

Composed function | Inner function | Output of inner function | Outer function | Input of outer function | Outer function has the right input?
- | - | - | - | - | -
$E(D(x))$ | $D$ | Hours of daylight | $E$ | Hours of daylight | Yes
$D(E(k))$ | $E$ | Amount of solar energy gained over the day | $D$ | The time of year | No
$E(G(r))$ | $G$ | Hours of video games Rafael can play | $E$ | Hours of daylight | No
$G(E(k))$ | $E$ | Amount of solar energy gained over the day | $G$ | Amount of solar energy | Yes

Neither $D(E(k))$ nor $E(G(r))$ get inputs that are within their domain, so neither makes sense in this context.

In conclusion, the following two composed functions make sense in this context:

- $E(D(x))$
- $G(E(k))$

assessment_items.question_content:
Li Na studied her farm, where she modeled the following relationships.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$S$| Amount of rice noodles produced, $t$ | Consumption of soy sauce, $S(t)$
$N$ | Amount of rice grown, $k$ | Amount of rice noodles produced, $N(k)$
$R$ | Amount of rainfall in centimeters, $x$ | Amount of rice grown, $R(x)$

**Which $2$ of the following composed functions make sense in this context?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The inner function's output becomes the input to the outer function. So the composition will only make sense if the output of the inner function matches the input of outer function.

Let's look at whether the outer function gets the correct input.

Composed function | Inner function | Output of inner function | Outer function | Input of outer function | Outer function has the right input?
- | - | - | - | - | -
$S(N(k))$ | $N$ | Amount of rice noodles produced | $S$ | Amount of rice noodles produced | Yes
$N(R(x))$ | $R$ | Amount of rice grown | $N$ | Amount of rice grown | Yes
$R(S(t))$ | $S$ | Consumption of soy sauce | $R$ | Amount of rainfall in centimeters | No
$N(S(t))$ | $S$ | Consumption of soy sauce | $N$ | Amount of rice grown | No

Neither $R(S(t))$ nor $N(S(t))$ get inputs that are within their domain, so neither makes sense in this context.

In conclusion, the following two composed functions make sense in this context:

- $S(N(k))$
- $N(R(x))$

assessment_items.question_content:
Burian is a very prolific writer, able to write thousands of pages per day! His assistant modeled a few functional relationships below.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$P$ | Minutes of morning yoga, $x$ | Number of pages Burian writes, $P(x)$
$I$ | Number of pages Burian writes, $k$ | Number of ink cartridges used, $I(k)$
$S$| Number of ink cartridges used, $r$ | Number of times the assistant has to walk to the office supply shop, $S(r)$

**Which $2$ of the following composed functions make sense in this context?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The inner function's output becomes the input to the outer function. So the composition will only make sense if the output of the inner function matches the input of outer function.

Let's look at whether the outer function gets the correct input.

Composed function | Inner function | Output of inner function | Outer function | Input of outer function | Outer function has the right input?
- | - | - | - | - | -
$S(P(x))$ | $P$ | Number of pages Burian writes | $S$ | Number of ink cartridges used | No
$I(P(x))$ | $P$ | Number of pages Burian writes | $I$ | Number of pages Burian writes | Yes
$S(I(k))$ | $I$ | Number of ink cartridges used | $S$ | Number of ink cartridges used | Yes
$P(I(k))$ | $I$ | Number of ink cartridges used | $P$ | Minutes of morning yoga | No

Neither $S(P(x))$ nor $P(I(k))$ get inputs that are within their domain, so neither makes sense in this context.

In conclusion, the following two composed functions make sense in this context:

- $I(P(x))$
- $S(I(k))$

assessment_items.question_content:
Denis has modeled the following relationships about a party he is planning.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$N$ | Dollars saved for the party, $x$ | Number of people Denis can invite to the party, $N(x)$
$M$ | Hours spent babysitting, $h$ | Dollars saved for the party, $M(h)$
$C$| Number of people invited to the party, $p$ | Cost of the party, $C(p)$

**Which $2$ of the following composed functions make sense in this context?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The inner function's output becomes the input to the outer function. So the composition will only make sense if the output of the inner function matches the input of outer function.

Let's look at whether the outer function gets the correct input.

Composed function | Inner function | Output of inner function | Outer function | Input of outer function | Outer function has the right input?
- | - | - | - | - | -
$N(C(p))$ | $C$ | Cost of the party | $N$ | Dollars saved for the party | No
$M(N(h))$ | $N$ | Number of people Denis can invite to the party | $M$ | Hours spent babysitting | No
$N(M(h))$ | $M$ | Dollars saved for the party | $N$ | Dollars saved for the party | Yes
$C(N(x))$ | $N$ | Number of people Denis can invite to the party | $C$ | Number of people Denis can invite to the party | Yes

Neither $N(C(p))$ nor $M(N(h))$ get inputs that are within their domain, so neither makes sense in this context.

In conclusion, the following two composed functions make sense in this context:

- $N(M(h))$
- $C(N(x))$

assessment_items.question_content:
Wang Xiu Ying is gift wrapping cube-shaped boxes for her class fundraiser. She has modeled the following relationships.

Function  | Input | Output
- | - | -
$S$ | Side length of box, $m$ | Surface area of box, $S(m)$
$P$ | Surface area of box, $a$ | Amount of wrapping paper needed, $P(a)$
$R$| Surface area of box, $x$ | Length of ribbon needed, $R(x)$

**Which $2$ of the following composed functions make sense in this context?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The inner function's output becomes the input to the outer function. So the composition will only make sense if the output of the inner function matches the input of outer function.

Let's look at whether the outer function gets the correct input.

Composed function | Inner function | Output of inner function | Outer function | Input of outer function | Outer function has the right input?
- | - | - | - | - | -
$R(P(a))$ | $P$ | Amount of wrapping paper needed | $R$ | Surface area of box | No
$S(R(x))$ | $R$ | Length of ribbon needed | $S$ | Side length of box | No
$P(S(m))$ | $S$ | Surface area of box | $P$ | Surface area of box | Yes
$R(S(m))$ | $S$ | Surface area of box | $R$ | Surface area of box | Yes

Neither $R(P(a))$ nor $S(R(x))$ get inputs that are within their domain, so neither makes sense in this context.

In conclusion, the following two composed functions make sense in this context:

- $P(S(m))$
- $R(S(m))$
